coredata.dc:title: New development in carbon quantum dots technical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Li-Ming
Liu, Jing
coredata.dc:description: As a newly emerged member in carbon nanomaterials family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attracted everincreasing attention owing to their ultracompact size, excellent photoluminescence, favorable biocompatibility, versatile surface and superior electron transfer ability. The past decade has witnessed continuous advancements in the production of CQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yields for various applications. Herein, we track the newest development of CQDs with advanced physicochemical properties and their applications in sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine and catalysis, and propose the challenges and perspectives in this exciting and promising field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Physicochemical properties
Sensing
Imaging
Nanomedicine
Catalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 81471308 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (Grant no. 2014023052 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of detection strategy for ACP activity based on CQDs nanoswitch triggered by PPi. (B) Schematic illustration of the detection strategy for PPi and ALP activity based on aggregation and disaggregation of carbon quantum dots. (C) Schematic illustration of the detection strategy for ALP activity based on aggregation and disaggregation of CQDs.
The fabrication procedure of ECL immunosensor for HIgG (A) and alpha fetoprotein (B).
The fabrication procedures for Paper-based electrochemiluminescence origami device for protein detection using assembled cascade DNA-CQDs nanotags based on rolling circle amplification.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of m-CQDs (a), o-CQDs (b) and p-CQDs (c) at a single λ=405nm laser excitation of MCF-7 cells; (B) In vivo fluorescence imaging of nude mice injected subcutaneously with CQD-EDA (2/1), CQD-DETA (2/1), and CQD-TEPA (2/1) at three injection sites (blue arrow) with increased doses (ai, bi, ci). The true color (TC) fluorescent composite images of CQDs fluorescence and autofluorescence (AF) from mice (aii, bii, cii), unmixed (UM) images of CQDs fluorescence (aiii, biii, ciii). Fluorescent intensities measured in region of interest (ROIs). Reproduced from [17,76] with permission. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Representation of solar H2 production using the hybrid CQD-NiP system. Irradiation of photoluminescent CQDs results in the direct transfer of photoexcited electrons to the catalyst NiP with subsequent reduction of aqueous protons. The electron donor EDTA carries out quenching of photoinduced holes in the CQDs.
The performance of optical sensors constructed by CQDs.

row.entry: Lake water, tap water, living cells
River water, fountain water, tap water
Triethylenetetramine-N, N, Nʹ, Nʹʹ, Nʹʹʹ, Nʹʹʹʹ-hexaacetic acid

head.ce:title: New development in carbon quantum dots technical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Regenerative Medicine Center, First Affiliated Hospital, Dalian Medical University, Dalian 116021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Regenerative Medicine Center, First Affiliated Hospital, Dalian Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a newly emerged member in carbon nanomaterials family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attracted everincreasing attention owing to their ultracompact size, excellent photoluminescence, favorable biocompatibility, versatile surface and superior electron transfer ability. The past decade has witnessed continuous advancements in the production of CQDs with high photoluminescence quantum yields for various applications. Herein, we track the newest development of CQDs with advanced physicochemical properties and their applications in sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine and catalysis, and propose the challenges and perspectives in this exciting and promising field.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles below 10nm [1]. CQDs possess comparable photoluminescence (PL) properties as semiconductor quantum dots, while CQDs outperform their counterpart in low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, a great selection of simple synthetic routes and apt to surface functionalization. By virtue of the superior physicochemical properties, CQDs triggered large scale studies on their synthesis, properties and technical applications in the fields of optical sensing, electrochemical sensing, imaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis. The exploration of effective synthetic pathways for CQDs was the focus around 2010. To sum up, synthetic approaches for CQDs comprise “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The former refers to the breakage of bulk carbon materials, such as graphite [2,3], carbon nanotubes [1,4], carbon soot [5] and activated carbon [6] via arc discharge, laser ablation, chemical and electrochemical oxidation. On the other hand, the “bottom-up” approaches produce CQDs from molecular precursors such as citrate [7], carbohydrates [8], and diverse biomass (soy milk, grass et al.) [9,10] by means of hydrothermal, microwave and calcination. Owing to the diverse crude materials and processing methods, CQDs consist of amorphous to nanocrystalline cores and a variety of surface functional groups. The fabrication of CQDs involves oxidation in general, so considerable amounts of oxygen-containing moieties are introduced, like carboxyl, carbonyl and hydroxyl. By elaborate selection of precursor bPEI25k, we prepared CQDs abundant with amine groups [11]. Richness in hydrophilic functional groups endow CQDs with excellent water dispersity and provide potential for further functionalization and surface passivation with various organic, inorganic or biological molecules. Surface functionalization modifies CQDs physical properties, like solubility in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents [12], photoluminescence properties (quantum yield (QY) increased 24.5 times after organic amine-graft [13]), and also improves the flexibility of CQDs, and further expands their application fields. Interestingly, the QY was below 10% initially, but increased up to 60–75.8% [14–18] by constant improvement of precursor, surface passivating reagent and preparation method.

The main composition elements of CQDs are C, H and O; moreover, heteroatom doping into CQDs, like boron [19], nitrogen [11] and sulfur [20], have been becoming a powerful way to improve the properties of CQDs, especially nitrogen doped CQDs (NCQDs) are quite popular. A range of NCQDs carrying 9.8–17% of nitrogen were produced by hydrothermal carbonization linear polyethylenic amine (PEA) analogues and citric acid. By adjusting the input amount of PEA, the structures of products altered along, and it's demonstrated the content of conjugated π-domains with C=N in the carbon backbone was correlated with their PL quantum yield [17].

Mechanistically, both the optical absorption and fluorescence emissions in CQDs do not originate from a band gap, but instead are due to π-plasmon and radiative recombination of the surface-confined electrons and holes, which is different from semiconductor quantum dots [21]. The emission wavelength and QY can be tuned by the size, excitation wavelength and structure through rationally changing the reaction parameters, such as the reagent's composition/ratio and reaction time/temperature. Unique size-dependent photoluminescence features of CQDs intrigued the exploration of size-tunable synthesis methods. Claverie [22] has recently reported an efficient approach to regulate CQDs size by varying the amount or structure of the precursor. Poly (acryloyl glucosamine) (acrylic acid) copolymer (P(AGA)(AA)) formed reverse micelles in heptanol first and then was graphitized under 170°C to prepare CQDs. Increasing the number of carbon precursor AGA units in the polymer chain led to glucose enrichment in the reverse micelle, and thus results in a larger CQDs particle size. The obtained 3.4, 2.9, and 2.2nm CQDs exhibit blue, yellow and red fluorescence under 365nm UV light excitation. Excitation-dependent emission is another distinctive property of CQDs, and this feature was utilized in multicolor imaging. Photoluminescence can also be modulated by the structure of CQDs. CQDs emitting five colors (blue, green, yellow, orange and red) under white light excitation were produced by adjustment of the reagents and synthesis conditions [23]. Reaction parameters alternation affected nucleation and growth process and generated dots with either more oxygen-containing or nitrogen containing groups that ultimately determine their photoluminescence properties.

Photoexcited CQDs are proven to be both excellent electron donors and electron acceptors, since either electron acceptor or electron donor molecules disrupt the radiative recombinations of CQDs [24], and then induce PL quenching. We applied this finding in reduction noble metal salts into the corresponding metal nanoparticles (Ag-NPs/Au-NPs) coating on the surface of CQDs [25,26]. These interesting electron transfer properties offer new opportunities in using CQDs for light energy conversion related applications.

As a rising star in carbon nanomaterial family, more creative investigations in CQDs are underway. Not only the excellent PL properties of CQDs find their application in sensing and imaging, but also the superior electron transfer ability expanded their use in catalysis. There are already several excellent review articles on synthesis, properties and applications of CQDs [27,28]. Herein, this article updates the latest progress in this rapidly evolving field and place emphases on their main physicochemical features and technical applications along with discussion on challenges and perspectives in this exciting and promising field.

The performance of optical sensors constructed by CQDs is summarized in Table 1. The optical properties of CQDs are sensitive to their surface states, therefore the change of surrounding environment would affect their fluorescence. Nonradiative electron-transfer between CQDs and metal ions cause fluorescence quenching of CQDs, and this fact was extensively exploited to precisely determine multiple metallic ion. Hg2+ represents a serious threat to the environment and human health due to its high toxicity and easy accumulation in human body; hence the sensitive and efficient approaches for Hg2+ quantification are urgently required. Several groups investigated the practicability of CQDs with high photoluminescence for label-free detection of Hg2+. Simultaneous doping of N and S effectively promoted electron-transfer and coordination interaction between N, S/CQDs and Hg2+, and this facilitates the selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ in lake water, tap water and living cells [20]. On the other hand, a sensitive analysis method for Hg2+ with broad linear range of 0.01–100 μM, and detection limit of 5.5nM, was constructed based on static quenching of CQDs fluorescence. Compared to other metal ions, high selectivity of CQDs is ascribed to the fact that Hg2+ has stronger binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with amino and carboxylic groups on the surfaces of CQDs [29]. Hou [30] exploited CQDs made from electrochemical carbonization as a label-free probe for Hg2+ sensing with detection limit of 3.3nM. Surface engineering of CQDs based on the chemically reactive groups broaden their applications in multiple area. Specific recognition molecules functionalized CQDs improve sensing sensitivity and selectivity remarkably. Nitrogen-doped CQDs functionalized with dithiothreitol, which has strong affinity for Hg2+ binding, effectively reduced the detection limit to 18 pM [31]. Similarly, the quenching process was widely employed to construct optical sensor to detect Fe3+ [29,32] and K+ [33] with great performance.

In addition to metallic ions, the determination of tetracycline (Tc) [34], free chlorine [35] and NADH [36] were also realized based on PL decrease of CQDs. The non-fluorescent intermediate formed via nucleophilic addition between electron-deficient C=O on CQDs surface and the electron-rich phenolic oxygen anion of chlorophenolate was found out to be responsible for the quenching of CQDs and this facilitates ultrasensitive detection of chlorophenols [37]. The comparable quantitative detection results of four different concentration of catechol in PBS are obtained by NCQDs sensing probe and HPLC [38]. As a versatile probe, CQDs made by hydrothermal treatment of ethanol in H2O2 solution can detect pH, temperature and ClO− simultaneously [39]. The PL intensity of CQDs declined dramatically as pH increased from 3 to 11. Based on this feature, a CQDs coated fluorescent paper for visual detection of pH by naked eyes has been successfully manufactured. All in all, abundant surface groups facilitate the coordination between CQDs and analytes, and their interaction leads to fluorescence quenching which was exploited to build multifunctional analytical system.

When the absorption of analytes overlap with the fluorescence emission of CQDs, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) induced quenching of CQDs and this was successfully applied in detection of auramine O [40], Although FRET process between CQDs and hemoglobin (Hb) was already used to construct a sensing platform for Hb with detection limit estimated to be 34nM, the addition of H2O2 reduced the LOD further to 0.4nM, because iron released from Hb induced Fenton reaction and reacted with H2O2 to generate reactive oxygen species including hydroxyl (∙OH) and super oxide (O2∙−) radicals which cause more profound quenching effect on CQDs [41]. In the same way, accurate determination of H2O2 and glucose in serum was realized by means of Fenton reaction directly [42].

To further improve sensing sensitivity and selectivity, metal ions, organic compounds and polymer functionalized CQDs nanohybrids have been designed and used in the detection of specific analytes. Fe3+-functionalized CQDs were utilized to measure ascorbic acid (AA) in rat brain microdialysates based on the specific redox reaction between Fe3+ and AA [43]. Tyrosinase activity monitoring and inhibitor screening was actualized by dopamine-functionalized CQDs on account of the exclusive reaction between dopamine and tyrosinase [44]. The fluorescence quenching and enhancement of DTPA-Eu conjugated CQDs corresponded to the binding of Tc and H2O2 respectively. Therefore, with rational design and surface engineering, sequential imaging of intracellular Tc and H2O2 in Plat-E cells was implemented [45]. Engineering CQDs surface with ʟ-tyrosine methyl ester facilitates the selective determination of organophosphorus pesticides residue in cabbage, milk and fruit juice [46]. Ni found out FAM-DNA assembled on the surface of CQDs have an ultra-sensitive response towards 6-mercaptopurine [47]. The combination of great fluorescence property of CQDs with selectivity of molecular imprinted polymers (MIP) was applied to detect glucose [48] and α-amanitin [49]. CQDs embedded in MIP were used as antennae for selective recognition signal amplification and optical readout, and the MIPs shell allowed only specific adsorption, thereby preventing any interfering species from coming into contact with CQDs. Notably, the highly stable immobilization of CQDs in the gel networks provides the hybrid microgels with excellent optical signal reproducibility after five repeated cycles of addition and dialysis removal of glucose in the bathing medium.

Advanced “off-on” fluorescence assay based on the formation and dissociation of metal ions/CQDs complex was utilized to sense Fe3+ and phytic acid [50], Au3+ and glutathione [51], Hg2+ and I− [16], Hg2+ and biothiols/histidine [52]. First, the fluorescence of CQDs was significantly quenched by metal ions via the formation of complex. Upon addition of analytes, which has stronger affinity for metal ions, CQDs were released, accompany with the recovery of fluorescence. Moreover, Feng established a series of fluorescence nanoswitch with a mode of “off-on-off” to assess enzyme activity (as shown in Fig. 1), such as acid phosphatase (ACP) [53] and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) [54,55]. By rational selection of chemical oxidation synthesis pathway, the obtained CQDs abundant in carboxyl groups can easily complex with metal ions, and then severe aggregation of CQDs resulted in effective fluorescence quenching. With stronger affinity towards metal ions, pyrophosphateanions (PPi) broke up the combination by competitive binding with metal ions along with recovery of fluorescence. The introduction of ACP or ALP broke up the metal ions-PPi complex by means of hydrolyzing PPi. Therefore, the released metal ions in the solution aggregated CQDs once again, inducing decrease of PL, and this principle was applied to evaluate the activity of ACP or ALP.

When in contact with other nanomaterials, such as MnO2 nanosheets [56], MnO2 nanoflowers [57], CoOOH nanoflakes [58] or AuNPs [59,60], CQDs’ fluorescence was remarkably quenched. Upon the addition of analyte, which could degrade or destroy the quencher, CQDs were then released. CQDs-MnO2 nanocomposites synthesized through a facile one-step approach showed sensitive and selective response towards GSH [56]. Tris-derived CQDs hybridized with CoOOH nanoflakes resulted in pronounced quenching of CQDs via FRET. The specific redox reaction between the enediol group of AA and hexagonal CoOOH nanoflakes resulted in the collapse and decomposition of CoOOH from CQDs. The restored fluorescence from CQDs was applied as report signal for determination of cortical AA in rat brain microdialysates during the cerebral calm/ischemia process [58]. As is known, Au NPs quench fluorophore emission through the nonradioactive electronic excitation energy transfer from the fluorescent probe to Au NPs. Assembly of CQDs onto the surface of AuNPs through the Au-N interaction led to the decrease of fluorescence. With introduction of cysteine, the competition between -SH and nitrogen-containing groups to AuNPs caused desorption of CQDs. Meanwhile, the disaggregation of AuNPs occurred. Therefore, both up- and down-conversional PL intensity of CQDs restored and the solution changed from red to purple. On the other hand, the aggregated CQDs/AuNPs did not show any obvious change after adding glutathione or homocysteine. Therefore, a biosensor for sensitive and selective detection of cysteine by three modes including fluorescence measurements and colorimetric analysis was constructed. This multiple signals sensing system was successfully applied for the selective and accurate detection of cysteine in human serum with the detection limit of 4nM [59]. The distance between amino-functionalized CQDs and negatively charged hyaluronate stabilized AuNPs was drawn close by electrostatic interaction, and then surface energy transfer resulted in PL intensity decrease. However, hyaluronidase degraded the surface capping reagent of AuNPs and led to the collapse of nanohybrides. Based on the above mentioned experimental phenomena, the hyaluronidase level changes can be monitored simultaneously by the fluorescence emission intensity and visible light absorption values of the assay system [60].

Although fluorescence quenching and recovery-based sensing methods have attracted great interest in practical applications, it is still subjected to be easily affected by a variety of factors, such as probe concentration, excitation intensity, instrumental efficiency and measurement condition. On the contrary, ratiometric fluorescence methods use two emissions for self-calibration and are less prone to suffer such problems; additionally, the ratiometric probe can realize visualization of the analyte on the basis of two fluorescence intensity ratios for convenience. A novel nanohybrid ratiometric probe comprised of blue colored fluorescence CQDs and red-colored emission CdTe quantum dots (QDs) has been designed for on-site and visual determination of nitrogen dioxide [61]. Such hybridized probe exhibits dual emission bands centered at 460 and 665nm, respectively. The blue fluorescence of CQDs is insensitive to the analyte, whereas the red emission of QDs is specifically quenched by the analyte, resulting in a distinguishable color change from orange-red to blue upon exposure to NO2. The limit of detection for nitrogen dioxide is estimated to be 19nM in aqueous solution. More importantly, the nanohybrid probe has been successfully applied in visual detection of gaseous NO2 with a detection limit of 1ppm, suggesting its potential real world application for NO2 sensing. CQDs with surface carboxyl and amine groups served both as a scaffold for coordination with Tb3+ and a fluorescence reference, while Tb3+ act as the receptor and response unit to recognize dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an anthrax biomarker. What's more, CQDs reference and DPA sensitized Tb3+ ions can be excited with one single wavelength, but their fluorescence emissions do not interfere each other [62]. With blue emissive CQDs serving as reference fluorophore and red emissive 11-mercaptoundecanoic acid capped AuNCs as a specific recognition unit, the CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid show specific response towards Cd2+ as low as 32.5nM attributed to the static quenching and the inner filter effect of Cd2+ to CQDs/AuNCs nanohybrid. In combination with “on-off-on” mode, this optical sensor regained its fluorescence emission upon addition of AA [63]. In the above mentioned cases, CQDs function as reference fluorophore owing to their stable physicochemical and optical properties.

Carbonaceous materials are ideal electrode material owing to their electrochemical activity. CQDs also show excellent electrochemical properties, and this property opens new avenue for their application in detecting electroactive molecules. NCQDs were loaded on glassy carbon electrode, and the different pulse voltammetry response of the modified electrode indicated that the oxidation peak current improves markedly with increasing the concentration of dopamine in PBS [64,65]. A dual sensing platform based on CQDs sensitive fluorescent and electrochemical response towards trinitrotoluene was established. The minimum distinguishable response concentration with respect to these two methods is down to the nanomolar level with a high specificity and sensitivity [66].

Interestingly, the photoelectrochemical feature of CQDs was newly explored in sensing. Resonance energy transfer between the CQDs and AuNPs-Cys (CQDs-AuNPs-Cys conjugates) promoted charge separation and interfacial photoinduced electron transfer which led to significant photovoltaic conversion efficiency and amplification of the photoelectrochemical signal. The combination of HeLa cells with CQDs-AuNPs-Cys conjugates via the specific affinity between folic acid and the folic acid receptor hindered the electron donor to electrode surface, decreasing the photoelectrochemical response. Thus, a novel signal-off photoelectrochemical cytosensor at low potential was proposed based on the difference in photocurrent intensity before and after capturing HeLa cells [67] ( Fig. 2).

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) signals originate from the excited states of an ECL luminophore generated at the electrode surfaces back to ground states during the electrochemical reaction. CQDs exhibit ECL activity and the ECL signal of CQDs was intensified a lot in the presence of coreactant K2S2O8 [68]. Wei noticed NCQDs displayed stronger and more stable ECL signals compared to pure CQDs, due to easier electro-reduction of NCQDs generating more abundant NCQDs∙−. They deposited NCQDs on electrode surface and construct immunosensor for specific detection of HIgG on the basis of ECL quenching due to immunological recognition between antibody and HIgG (as shown in Fig. 2A) [69]. On the other hand, aminated graphene labelled with secondary antibody was introduced to the analytical system and constituted sandwich-type ECL immunosensor enabling the quantitative determination of alpha fetoprotein. ECL resonance energy transfer between CQDs and aminated graphene on electrode surface induced the decline of ECL signals (Fig. 2B). In order to further validate the proposed immunosensor, a comparison with the commercialized available enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay method was carried out and these two results are in good agreement, further indicating the feasibility of the proposed immunosensor for clinical application [70]. Immunological recognition in combination with rolling circle amplification (RCA) technique was utilized to construct a paper-based ECL origami device for determination of HIgG. As indicated in Fig. 3, upon the binding of secondary antibodies-gold nanoparticles conjugated with primer strands on electrodes, the RCA was initialized. The RCA product containing tandem-repeat sequences served as an excellent template for periodic assembly of oligonucleotide functionalized CQDs, which presented per protein recognition event to numerous CQDs tags for ECL readout. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed strategy showed remarkable amplification efficiency, very little nonspecific adsorption with good stability, reproducibility and accuracy [71].

Cytotoxicity effects play a key role in determining the applicability of nanoparticles as imaging probes. As for this, CQDs are mainly composed of C, H, O, N or S, and thus certain amount of CQDs caused no harm to cells and organism. It has been demonstrated CQDs are nontoxic, certainly without the toxicity concerns associated with the heavy metal-containing semiconductor QDs. Therefore, there has been a growing interest in potential applications of CQDs for fluorescence bioimaging in vitro and in vivo. High quantum yield of CQDs can guarantee the sensitivity of imaging. Besides, characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of CQDs made multicolor imaging possible. After decoration with recognition moiety, CQDs can only discriminate target cells, enhance imaging quality and drug delivery efficiency.

Wang investigated the cytotoxicity of CQDs on HeLa and MCF-7 cells respectively. It turned out that the cell viability of >90% (HeLa cells) and >94% (MCF-7 cells) were achieved at a CQDs concentration of <5.0mgmL−1 and incubating duration for 6h. When excited by 340 and 495nm, bright blue and green emissions from HeLa and MCF-7 cells are clearly recorded by fluorescent microscope which correlated well with the characteristic wavelength-dependent emission of CQDs [72]. 400μgmL−1 CQDs made from C3N4 and ethane diamine incubating with HeLa cells for 48h caused no significant reduction in cell viability [38]. Biocompatibility and bioimaging performance of CQDs have also been evaluated on lung normal (L-132) and cancer (A549) cell lines, and MTT results indicated that the survival rate of A549 and L-132 cells was around 85% even at a high concentration of 1mgmL−1 CQDs. CQDs were ubiquitously distributed in the cytoplasm of A549 and L-132 cells, evident from the green fluorescence emanating around the nucleus in fluorescence microscopic images. The quantification of cellular uptake of CQDs in A549 and L-132 cells measured by flow cytometer showed the percentage of fluorescent cells in treated cells increased correspondingly in a dose-dependent manner [73]. Another immortalized non-tumerogenic human keratinocyte cell, HaCaT cell, was chosen to test the cytotoxicity of cabbage derived CQDs. The results indicated relative cell viability of HaCaT was still more than 90% after 24h exposure in CQD at a concentration below 700μgmL−1. Meanwhile, CQDs treated cells displayed three distinguished blue, green and red colors in cell membrane and cytoplasm when excited with 405, 488 and 543nm laser respectively [74]. It's noteworthy that the fluorescence of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs prepared from garlic by a hydrothermal method are resistant to the interference of metal ions, biomolecules and high ionic strength environments, which were used as excellent candidate for cellular multicolor imaging [75]. More interestingly, three isomers of phenylenediamines, o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, and p-phenylenediamine, was selected as precursors for producing CQDs through a facile solvothermal method, which displayed green, blue and red fluorescence respectively under single wavelength UV irradiation. The unique optical features of these three CQDs with low cytotoxicity made them potent multiplexed bioimaging reagents. After MCF-7 cells incubating with each of the three CQDs for 4h, the live cells showed different color emissions under one single 405nm laser excitation (Fig. 4A) [76].

Bare CQDs can easily penetrate cell membrane and usually settle in cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area [15,32], when conjugated with recognition factors, the targeting ability of CQDs could be properly regulated. CQDs made from folic acid and urea through microwave carry folic acid and specifically target the cells overexpressing folate receptor. This feature was applied to distinguish FA-receptor-positive cancer cells from normal cells [77]. On the other hand, strong blue fluorescent CQDs (QY 75.8%) functionalized with a nuclear localization signal peptide could be internalized in the nucleus of MCF7 and A549 cells and emit blue light during intracellular localization experiments [18].

Fluorescent CQDs were conjugated onto magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (Fe3O4@mSiO2) surface through triphenylphospine (TPP) to fabricate Fe3O4@mSiO2-TPP/CQDs nanoplatform. The as-prepared nanoplatform shows low cytotoxicity and apoptosis rate in various cell lines, such as A549, CHO, HeLa, SH-SY5Y, HFF and HMEC-1. More importantly, this nanoplatform integrates long time cell imaging, mitochondria-targeting, and magnetic field-enhanced cellular uptake functionalities into an all-in-one system. Time-dependent mitochondrial colocalization in multiple cell lines has been proved by using confocal laser scanning microscopy and flow cytometry, meanwhile the multicolored fluorescence of the Fe3O4@mSiO2-TPP/CQDs could remain bright and stable in cells for 24h [78]. A dual-modal fluorescence/magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) nanoprobe was constructed by surface conjugation of CQDs with cyclic DTPA dianhydride (cDTPAA) and further Gd3+ chelation. It's noted that the longitudinal relaxivity of CQDs-DTPA-Gd was calculated to be 56.72mM−1 s−1, which is much higher than commercially available MRI agent Gadovist (r1=4.34mM−1 s−1) [79].

In addition to cell imaging, CQDs have also been applied in bacterial labelling and detection. The attachment of amphiphilic CQDs to bacteria enables their detection through both fluorescence spectroscopy and microscopy. Importantly, the intensity and spectral position of the CQDs’ fluorescence varied with bacterial species, providing a tool for distinguishing among bacteria even in cases of mixed bacterial populations. Moreover, after labelling with CQDs, bacterial physiological processes such as cell division could be clearly visualized [80]. Mannose-modified fluorescent CQDs could selectively bind to the FimH lectin unit in the flagella of the wild-type 1 Escherichia coli K12 strain. Based on this, a quantitative detection method for the bacteria at levels down to 450 colony forming units mL−1 in lab samples was set up. Also, the accurate analyses of bacteria in real samples (tap water, apple juice, and human urine) validated the practicability of CQDs [81]. Additionally, CQDs modified with vancomycin showed obvious and specific affinity with Gram-positive bacteria owing to the ligand-receptor interactions between vancomycin and the cell walls. Compared with conventional plate counting detection methods, which generally requires time consuming and sophisticated operations, CQDs labelling methodology is more convenient, fast and sensitive [82].

Good biosafety of nanomaterial is a prerequisite for use in vivo, while preliminary biocompatibility evaluations of fluorescent CQDs focused on cytotoxicity. Recently, Liu first evaluated the hemocompatibility of CQDs and found out CQDs at ≤0.1mg/mL had few adverse effects on the blood components, but at higher doses, CQDs impair the structure and function of the blood components. These results provide valuable information for further clinical application [83]. In vitro and in vivo bioimaging was actualized simultaneously with high performance photoluminescent CQDs. When excited and detected at 535nm and 695–770nm respectively, fluorescence signal could be readily visualized after 0.5 and 5mg of CQDs were injected into athymic BALB/c-nu mice (Fig. 4(B)). Significant benefits of CQDs in in vivo optical imaging were attributed to far red emission wavelengths which provide better penetration ability than visible light [17]. Multicolor in vivo fluorescence imaging of zebrafish embryo with CQDs as probe was presented by Yin. After soaking with CQDs, the development of embryo from single cell to larva was tracked by fluorescence imaging. Results indicated that CQDs have different tissue affinities. When the concentration of CQDs was at 1.5 or 1.25mgmL−1, the livability of embryos or larva treated by CQDs solution for a period of time are both higher than 80%, which demonstrate the low toxicity and high biocompatibility of CQDs. By means of tracing fluorescent CQDs, the absorption, distribution, metabolism and excretion route of CQDs in zebrafish is revealed [84].

CQDs provide an excellent nanomaterial candidate for efficient theranostic applications. Luminescent CQDs with anionic terminus in combination with cationic acetylated G5 poly (amido amine) (G5-Ac85) dendrimers formed versatile nanoplatform integrating imaging and therapeutic modalities when encapsulating chemo-drug epirubicin (EPI) in the dendrimer interiors. Based on the green fluorescence of CQDs and red fluorescence of EPI, the as-prepared multifunctional CQDs@EPI⊂G5-Ac85 hybrids were used to image the cellular distribution of anticancer drug EPI in MCF-7 cells. Finally, the release of drug from hybrids resulted in cytotoxic effects in breast cancer cell [85]. bPEI25k is a golden standard transfection reagent, but suffer from high toxicity. Researchers investigated the employment of bPEI25k as passivation reagents or carbon source to fabricate cationic CQDs, and demonstrated the potent potential of the obtained CQDs serving as carriers for nucleic acid delivery in vitro and in vivo. Lebeau et al. selected citric acid as the carbon source and bPEI25k for in situ passivation to synthesize CQDs, and then studied the transfection efficiency of CQDs delivering plasmid DNA or small interfering RNA to various cell lines. The as-prepared cationic nanoparticles proved efficient for condensing DNA. Moreover, they discovered this brightly fluorescent nanoparticles exhibited in vitro transfection efficiency comparable to or better than control bPEI25k, but with lower toxicity. Finally, non-invasive administration of CQDs/DNA to mouse lungs achieved similar efficiency to that of GL67A, the golden standard formulation for pulmonary administration of DNA, but with evidently lower toxicity. In addition, post-functionalization of these CQDs with PEG or targeting moieties should remarkably broaden their scope and practical implications in improving in vivo transfection efficiency and biocompatibility [86].

Except for working as nanomedicine carrier, the intrinsic theranostic application of CQDs was discussed in vitro and in vivo. A novel red-emissive CQDs derived from conjugated polymer displayed characteristic emission peak at 640nm with a broad absorption band in the visible to NIR region (400–800nm). CQDs show high photothermal conversion efficiency (η=38.5%) under 671nm NIR laser irradiation. Meanwhile, CQDs are photoacoustic (PA) active agents. HeLa tumor-bearing nude mice model were treated with intravenous injections of CQDs. PL and PA imaging results both verified the accumulation of CQDs in tumor region owing to enhanced permeability and retention effect. PL and PA imaging-guided photothermal therapy caused substantial empyrosis at the tumor site, thereby suppressed tumor growth remarkably [87]. Due to the interaction with human insulin, CQDs prevent insulin from fibrillation for 5 days under 65°C, whereas insulin denatures in 3h under the same conditions without CQDs. The inhibiting insulin fibrillation effect opens up the possibility of CQDs application in the pharmaceutical industry for the processing and formulation of insulin [88].

For the efficient conversion of light energy into chemical energy, photocatalytically active nanomaterials have been employed to accelerate the reaction. The broadband light-absorption ability and superior electron transfer ability of CQDs has inspired their application in photocatalysis. Several hybrid photocatalytic systems with CQDs working as sensitizers have been studied and the enhanced activity was obtained, such as NCQDs/Bi2O3, CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/N-ZnO et al. NCQDs modified ordered porous Bi2O3 inverse opal structure (IOS) was mixed with Rhodamine and then exposed under simulated sunlight irradiation. Decolorization of Rhodamine was verified by rapid decrease of characteristic absorption peak at 553nm with time extension. Compared to Bi2O3, photocatalytic activity of NCQDs/Bi2O3 was 4 times higher owing to the improvement of light absorption intensity and electronic transmission properties of Bi2O3 IOS film induced by NCQDs [89]. CQDs modified BiOCl ultrathin nanosheets photocatalyst showed evidently enhanced photocatalytic performance as compared with pure BiOCl, based on more efficient degradation of bisphenol A and rhodamine B by hybrid materials under ultraviolet, visible and near infrared light irradiation. The crucial role of CQDs was mainly attributed to the superior electron transfer ability, enhanced light harvesting and boosted catalytic active sites [90]. A versatile CQDs/N-ZnO photocatalyst was able to degrade all three commercial dyes, malachite green, methylene blue and fluorescein dyes, within 30–45min, under daylight irradiation. Also, the reusability of CQDs/N-ZnO photocatalyst reached up to 4 times without significant deactivation due to anti-photocorrosion property of CQDs [91]. The synergetic effect of metallic oxide and CQDs facilitates the new class of photocatalyst for self-cleaning function and environmental remediation under naturally available daylight source.

The abovementioned photocatalytic applications of CQDs were all in conjunction with another photosensitizer and for photodegradation of organic dyes, while CQDs alone were found to be excellent photocatalysts with great potential very recently. According to the emission quenching and conductivity measurements, Cu-N doping resulted in the electron-accepting and -donating abilities of CQDs enhancing 2.5 and 1.5 times, and also the electric conductivity increasing to 171.8mscm−1. Owing to the promoted electron transfer properties, the photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs in the photooxidation reaction of 1,4-dihydro-2,6-dimethylpyridine-3,5-dicarboxylate was improved 3.5-fold after doping [92].

Reisner first reported a solar H2 production system using CQDs as the sole photosensitizer in combination with a molecular Ni catalyst ( Fig. 5). Irradiation of CQDs results in absorption of light and the direct transfer of photoexcited electrons to the catalyst NiP, which carries out reduction of aqueous protons. Afterwards, the photoinduced holes in the CQDs were effectively quenched by electron donor EDTA. Maximum activity parallels that of previously reported dye-catalyst hybrid systems, whereas the CQDs themselves display a photostability of more than 24h, thereby offering a distinct advantage over rapidly photodegrading molecular dye systems in aqueous solution [93]. Dunn et al. combined three biomass-derived CQDs (chitin-, chitosan-, and glucose-) with ZnO nanorods to fabricate solid-state nanostructured solar cells for the first time. The functional groups on CQDs promoted their binding with ZnO surface by electrostatic interactions. CQDs sensitize ZnO nanorods to visible light and the highest efficiency of 0.077% was achieved with a layer by layer coating of chitosan- and chitin- derived CQDs. Nevertheless, the efficiencies still require further improvement to provide a powerful technological alternative to current devices. The researchers speculated coating thicker layers of CQDs, or increasing the visible light absorption of the CQDs themselves by size or composition control would further improve the light-harvesting efficiency of the devices [94].

CQDs decorated with hydrogensulfate groups (SCQDs) possess photoinduced proton-generating capacity under visible light irradiation. As recyclable light-enhanced acid-catalysts, SCQDs catalyze ring-opening reactions of epoxides, and achieve high conversion efficiency at room temperature under visible light irradiation. The use of SCQDs as visible-light responsive and controllable photocatalysts is a novel application of carbon-based nanomaterials for green chemistry [95]. Polymer/N-doped CQD hybrids that covalently enclosed or confined NCQDs in polymers preserved the PL properties of pristine NCQDs and accelerated the photochemical reaction as those of photoreduction of silver ions to silver nanoparticles (under UV-light), selective photo-oxidation of benzylalcohol to the benzaldehyde (under vis-light) and the photocatalytic generation of H2 (under UV-light) [96].

The potential use of CQDs in photocatalytic schemes has been widely demonstrated, whereby they can absorb UV and visible light, and directly transfer photoexcited electrons to solution based molecular electron acceptors and catalysts. CQDs are shown to be a highly sustainable light-absorbing material for photocatalytic schemes, which are not limited by cost, toxicity or lack of scalability.

The development of doped carbonaceous materials has become a well-known strategy to replace the platinum-based catalysts in energy storage and conversion devices. The heteroatom (N, B, P or S) doped CQDs show outstanding performance for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), especially dual doped B,N-CQDs and P,N-CQDs exhibit comparable high catalytic activities as commercial Pt/C for the ORR [97]. The supernal conductivity, rapid electron transfer and electron reservoir properties make CQDs potent electrocatalyst. What's more, the abundant surface functional groups make it much easier to form composites and further enhance the catalytic activities of the original materials. Electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) are crucial for a variety of renewable energy applications and energy-intensive industries. In alkaline media, CQDs/SnO2-Co3O4 complex exhibited high electrocatalytic activity and long-term stability for efficient OER, which outperformed either pristine Co3O4 or SnO2-Co3O4 composite. Experimental results indicated that the Co atoms were considered as the active center, the nano-sized SnO2 enhanced electronic conductivity and the insoluble CQDs layer on the surface effectively protected the catalyst, otherwise the catalyst would dissolve in alkaline solution [98].

NCQDs also provide a promising way to tailor the transport properties of catalyst support for electronic applications. NCQDs-MWCNT working as a support of Pt-based anode catalyst was innovatively applied in direct methanol fuel cells. Pt nanoparticles are well dispersed onto NCQDs-MWCNT. The results of electrochemical measurements reveal that Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT catalyst exhibits 1.3 times higher activity for methanol electrooxidation than that of Pt/MWCNT. The better performance of Pt/NCQDs-MWCNT is attributed to the fact that NCQDs improve the dispersion of MWCNT and more uniform Pt nanoparticles are stabilized on NCQDs-MWCNT[99].

The counter electrode (CE) is an important component in solar cells and considerable efforts have been devoted to searching appropriate materials to fabricate CEs with lower resistances and higher reduction rates of the redox electrolyte. The counter electrode employing CQDs-Au nanoraspberries have higher electrocatalytic activity, lower charger-transfer resistance and larger exchange current density than commonly used Au-sputtered counter electrodes. The power conversion efficiency of the cell was improved to 5.4%, in comparison to efficiencies of 3.6% and 0.18% shown by cells employing Au sputtered and CQDs counter electrodes, respectively [100].

The exploration of CQDs working as light-emissive components in solid-state luminescent materials is always challenging, since aggregation of CQDs in solid-state matrices results in light reabsorption effects or the non-radiative decay processes. To overcome this obstacle, the suitable matrices in which CQDs can disperse homogeneously are of immense importance to retain high PL QY. Polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS), a unique cage-like molecule, offers such a potentially suitable matrix for CQDs dispersion. Water soluble N, S-co-doped CQDs was embedded into the POSS matrix, and this solid-state luminophores CQDs@POSS composite exhibits a record high PL quantum yield of 60% [14]. The development of solid-state luminophores promised a bright future in display and lighting. Sun took advantage of the excitation wavelength dependent PL feature of CQDs to fabricate a series of monochromatic light-emitting diodes which display whole visible range from blue to red. In practice, CQDs were mixed with transparent polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) in chloroform solution beforehand and then deposited dropwise in a layer-by-layer fashion onto the UV-LED chips. Upon deposition of more CQDs, the outer layer absorbed short wavelength light from the inner layer and reemitted bathochromic shift light. The emission color of LEDs can be easily regulated from the blue to the red with CQDs/PMMA layer thickness increase [101].

The applications of CQDs in optical sensing, electrochemical sensing, imaging and nanomedicine have already proved to play a big role in nanotechnology, and the practical application of CQDs in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis would be beneficial to address the energy and environmental problems. Until now, a good supply of investigation on CQDs provides valuable insights into their synthesis, properties and applications, while a great number of improvements are still required. Since the synthesis pathways and starting materials vary a lot, the properties of as-prepared CQDs thereupon change along, and controversial conclusion are often encountered with different kinds of CQDs. Usually we rely on morphology, optical characteristics and composition element to confirm the identity of CQDs which is not quite enough and accurate, especially after the discovery of graphene quantum dots, another similar photoluminescent carbon emitter, so the definition which can distinguish CQDs from any other materials are urgently needed. On the other hand, although the emission spectral of CQDs shift to red with the increase of excitation wavelength and can even cover the whole visible region, the emission intensity decreased along. Especially the emission in near-infrared region is quite weak, which hinders the application in biomedical field. In terms of efficiency for biological application, optimizing PL QY is essential for improving the fluorescence signal and consequently reducing the dosage and potential toxicity in bioimaging applications. In this case, the exploitation of valid routes to prepare red emissive CQDs still demands more in depth work. Semiconductor quantum dots with successive increasing size can be easily produced by prolonging reaction time, while the precise control of CQDs size like their counterpart hasn’t been realized by present technologies. In summary, more research is desired to better answer the above questions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 81471308) and Natural Science Foundation of Liaoning Province (Grant no. 2014023052).
